examples.explanation: Step 1: Build adjacency: 0в†’[1], 1в†’[2,3], 2в†’[0], 3в†’[]. Step 2: Find SCCs: Check pairs: (0,1): BFS 0в†’1 reachable? Yes (0в†’1). BFS 1в†’0 reachable? Yes (1в†’2в†’0). So 0,1 same SCC. (0,2): 0в†’2? Yes (0в†’1в†’2). 2в†’0? Yes (2в†’0). So 0,2 same SCC. (1,2): 1в†’2? Yes. 2в†’1? Yes (2в†’0в†’1). So 1,2 same SCC. (0,3): 0в†’3? Yes (0в†’1в†’3). 3в†’0? No. So different SCCs. (1,3): 1в†’3? Yes. 3в†’1? No. (2,3): 2в†’3? Yes (2в†’0в†’1в†’3). 3в†’2? No. Thus SCCs: {0,1,2} and {3}. Step 3: Build condensation: SCC0={0,1,2}, SCC1={3}. Check SCC0в†’SCC1: Any vertex in SCC0 reach vertex in SCC1? 0в†’3? Yes (0в†’1в†’3). Add edge 0в†’1. Check SCC1в†’SCC0: 3в†’0? No. Result: nodes 0,1 with edge 0в†’1.

Step 1: Adjacency: 0в†’[1], 1в†’[2], 2в†’[1]. Step 2: Find SCCs: (0,1): 0в†’1? Yes. 1в†’0? No. Different. (0,2): 0в†’2? Yes (0в†’1в†’2). 2в†’0? No. Different. (1,2): 1в†’2? Yes. 2в†’1? Yes. Same SCC. So SCCs: {0}, {1,2}. Step 3: Condensation: SCC0={0}, SCC1={1,2}. SCC0в†’SCC1: 0в†’1? Yes. Add edge 0в†’1. SCC1в†’SCC0: 1в†’0? No. 2в†’0? No. No edge. Result: nodes 0,1 with edge 0в†’1.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(VВІ + VE)
approaches.summary: For each pair of vertices, perform DFS/BFS to check if they are mutually reachable to identify SCCs. Then manually build condensation graph by checking connectivity between SCCs. This is conceptually simple but extremely inefficient for large graphs.

ladders.title: Full Problem вЂ” Brute Force Condensation Graph Construction
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of building DAG of SCCs using pairwise reachability checks
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute-force solution to the condensation graph problem. The intuition is straightforward: we need to identify strongly connected components (SCCs) where all vertices are mutually reachable, then build a directed acyclic graph (DAG) where each SCC becomes a node and edges represent reachability between SCCs. The brute-force approach works by checking every pair of vertices (u,v) to determine if they're in the same SCC (both uв†’v and vв†’u are reachable). Once SCCs are identified, we build the condensation graph by checking if any vertex in one SCC can reach any vertex in another SCC. The algorithm proceeds in three phases: 1) Build adjacency list from input edges. 2) For each vertex pair (u,v), perform BFS/DFS from u to check if v is reachable, and from v to check if u is reachable. If both are true, they belong to the same SCC. 3) After grouping vertices into SCCs, for each pair of SCCs (S_i, S_j), check if any vertex in S_i can reach any vertex in S_j via BFS/DFS. If yes, add edge S_iв†’S_j in condensation graph. Time complexity is O(VВІ + VE) because we perform O(VВІ) pairwise checks, each requiring O(V+E) BFS/DFS. Space complexity is O(V+E) for adjacency list and SCC storage. Connection to L1: This level integrates the SCC identification subroutine with condensation graph construction. Common mistakes include: forgetting to check both directions for SCC membership, not handling self-loops properly, and inefficiently re-running BFS/DFS for the same queries. Real code pattern: def condensation_graph_bruteforce(V, edges): adj = build_adjacency(V, edges); sccs = find_sccs_bruteforce(adj); return build_condensation(adj, sccs).

examples.trace: Phase 1: Build adjacency list. Phase 2: Pairwise mutual reachability checks identify SCCs [[0,1,2],[3]]. Phase 3: Check inter-SCC reachability в†’ add condensation edge [[0,1]].

Step 1: Adjacency: 0в†’[1], 1в†’[2], 2в†’[1]. Step 2: Find SCCs: (0,1): 0в†’1? Yes. 1в†’0? No. Different. (0,2): 0в†’2? Yes (0в†’1в†’2). 2в†’0? No. Different. (1,2): 1в†’2? Yes. 2в†’1? Yes. Same SCC. So SCCs: {0}, {1,2}. Step 3: Condensation: SCC0={0}, SCC1={1,2}. SCC0в†’SCC1: 0в†’1? Yes. Add edge 0в†’1. SCC1в†’SCC0: 1в†’0? No. 2в†’0? No. No edge. Result: nodes 0,1 with edge 0в†’1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine вЂ” Brute Force SCC Identification
ladders.desc: Identify all strongly connected components using pairwise reachability checks
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the brute-force approach, responsible for finding all SCCs in the graph. The intuition is that two vertices belong to the same SCC if and only if each is reachable from the other. We can determine this by checking every pair of vertices (u,v) and performing graph traversal from u to see if v is reachable, and from v to see if u is reachable. If both conditions hold, u and v are in the same SCC. The algorithm: 1) Build adjacency list from input edges. 2) Initialize an array component_id of size V, all set to -1. 3) For each vertex u from 0 to V-1: if component_id[u] == -1, assign it a new component ID and find all vertices v that are mutually reachable with u. To find mutually reachable vertices: perform BFS/DFS from u to get all vertices reachable from u (call this set R_u). For each vertex v in R_u: if v is not already assigned a component ID, perform BFS/DFS from v to check if u is in R_v (reachable from v). If yes, assign v the same component ID as u. 4) Group vertices by component ID to get SCC list. Time complexity is O(VВІ + VE) in worst case because we might perform BFS/DFS for O(VВІ) pairs. Space complexity is O(V+E) for adjacency and visited arrays. Connection to L0: This subroutine provides the SCC grouping needed for condensation graph construction. Common mistakes: not checking both directions properly, inefficiently re-computing reachability, and mishandling of visited states. Real code pattern: def find_sccs_bruteforce(adj): V=len(adj); comp_id=[-1]*V; curr_id=0; for u in range(V): if comp_id[u]==-1: comp_id[u]=curr_id; reachable_from_u=bfs(u,adj); for v in reachable_from_u: if comp_id[v]==-1 and u in bfs(v,adj): comp_id[v]=curr_id; curr_id+=1; return group_by_id(comp_id).

examples.trace: Step 1: Adjacency built. u=0: comp_id[0]=0. BFS from 0: reachable={0,1,2,3}. Then for each v in reachable, perform separate BFS from v to check if u=0 is reachable from v. v=1 and v=2 pass mutual check в†’ assigned to comp 0. v=3 fails. u=3 gets new component ID.

Step 1: Adjacency: 0в†’[1], 1в†’[2], 2в†’[1]. Step 2: comp_id=[-1,-1,-1], curr_id=0. u=0: comp_id[0]=0. BFS from 0: reachable={0,1,2}. Check v=1: comp_id[1]==-1, BFS from 1: reachable={1,2}. 0 in reachable? No. So comp_id[1] stays -1. v=2: comp_id[2]==-1, BFS from 2: reachable={1,2}. 0 in reachable? No. curr_id=1. u=1: comp_id[1]==-1, comp_id[1]=1. BFS from 1: reachable={1,2}. Check v=2: comp_id[2]==-1, BFS from 2: reachable={1,2}. 1 in reachable? Yes. comp_id[2]=1. curr_id=2. u=2: skip. Group: ID0: vertex 0; ID1: vertices 1,2.

ladders.title: Core Logic вЂ” Pairwise Mutual Reachability Checking
ladders.desc: Determine if two vertices are in the same SCC by checking bidirectional reachability
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique of the brute-force approach: determining whether two vertices belong to the same strongly connected component by checking if each is reachable from the other. The intuition stems from the definition of SCCs: a set of vertices where every pair of vertices is mutually reachable. For any two vertices u and v, if we can find a path from u to v AND a path from v to u, then they must be in the same SCC. The algorithm is straightforward: given a graph representation (adjacency list) and two vertices u and v, perform two graph traversals (BFS or DFS) вЂ“ one from u to see if v is reachable, and another from v to see if u is reachable. If both traversals succeed, return true; otherwise false. Time complexity for checking one pair is O(V+E) for each BFS/DFS, so O(V+E) total. Space complexity is O(V) for visited array and queue/stack. Connection to L1: This pairwise check is repeated for all vertex pairs to build complete SCC groupings. Common mistakes: not resetting visited array between traversals, confusing reachability with direct edge existence, and inefficiently re-running traversals for the same source vertex. Real code pattern: def are_mutually_reachable(adj, u, v): return bfs(u, adj, target=v) and bfs(v, adj, target=u). The BFS function returns True if target is reachable from source.

examples.trace: Step 1: BFS from u=0 to check if v=2 reachable. Queue: [0]. Visit 0, neighbors: [1]. Queue: [1]. Visit 1, neighbors: [2]. Queue: [2]. Visit 2, found target v=2. Reachable: true. Step 2: BFS from v=2 to check if u=0 reachable. Queue: [2]. Visit 2, neighbors: [0]. Queue: [0]. Visit 0, found target u=0. Reachable: true. Step 3: Both true, so mutually reachable.

Step 1: BFS from u=0 to v=2. Queue: [0]. Visit 0, neighbors: [1]. Queue: [1]. Visit 1, neighbors: [2]. Queue: [2]. Visit 2, found v=2. Reachable: true. Step 2: BFS from v=2 to u=0. Queue: [2]. Visit 2, neighbors: [1]. Queue: [1]. Visit 1, neighbors: [2]. Queue: [2] (already visited). BFS completes without finding u=0. Reachable: false. Step 3: One false, so not mutually reachable.

ladders.title: Building Block вЂ” Graph Reachability via BFS/DFS
ladders.desc: Check if a target vertex is reachable from a source vertex in a directed graph
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is fundamental to the brute-force approach: determining whether there exists a path from a source vertex to a target vertex in a directed graph. The intuition is that we can explore the graph systematically starting from the source, following outgoing edges, until we either find the target or exhaust all reachable vertices. Both BFS (breadth-first search) and DFS (depth-first search) work for this purpose. BFS explores neighbors level by level, while DFS goes deep first. The algorithm: 1) Initialize a visited boolean array of size V, all false. 2) Initialize a queue (for BFS) or stack (for DFS) with the source vertex. 3) While the queue/stack is not empty: pop a vertex, if it's the target, return true. Otherwise, mark it visited and push all its unvisited neighbors onto the queue/stack. 4) If queue/stack empties without finding target, return false. Time complexity is O(V+E) in worst case as we might visit all vertices and edges. Space complexity is O(V) for visited array and queue/stack. Connection to L2: This reachability check is performed twice (sourceв†’target and targetв†’source) to determine mutual reachability. Common mistakes: not marking vertices as visited when pushing to queue (causing infinite loops), forgetting to handle disconnected graphs, and confusing directed with undirected traversal. Real code pattern: def is_reachable(adj, source, target): visited=[False]*V; q=deque([source]); while q: node=q.popleft(); if node==target: return True; if not visited[node]: visited[node]=True; for neighbor in adj[node]: if not visited[neighbor]: q.append(neighbor); return False.

examples.trace: Step 1: visited=[F,F,F,F], q=[0]. Step 2: pop 0, **if not visited** mark visited[0]=T before processing neighbors (prevents duplicates), neighbors [1], q=[1]. Step 3: pop 1, mark visited[1]=T, neighbors [2], q=[2]. Step 4: pop 2, mark visited[2]=T, neighbors [3], q=[3]. Step 5: pop 3 equals target в†’ return true.

Step 1: visited=[F,F,F,F], q=[0]. Step 2: pop 0, not target, mark visited[0]=T, neighbors [1], q=[1]. Step 3: pop 1, not target, mark visited[1]=T, neighbors [2], q=[2]. Step 4: pop 2, not target, mark visited[2]=T, neighbors [1] but visited[1]=T, so skip, q=[]. Step 5: q empty, return false.

ladders.title: Basic Operation вЂ” Graph Traversal (BFS/DFS)
ladders.desc: Visit all vertices reachable from a starting vertex in a graph
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves systematically exploring all vertices that can be reached from a given starting vertex in a graph. The intuition is to start at the source and follow edges to discover connected vertices, ensuring we don't revisit vertices to avoid infinite loops. BFS uses a queue to explore neighbors level by level, visiting all direct neighbors first, then their neighbors, etc. DFS uses a stack (often via recursion) to go as deep as possible along one path before backtracking. The algorithm for BFS: 1) Initialize visited array of size V, all false. 2) Initialize queue with source vertex. 3) While queue not empty: pop vertex, if not visited, mark visited, process vertex (e.g., add to result list), and push all unvisited neighbors to queue. For DFS, replace queue with stack. Time complexity is O(V+E) as each vertex and edge is processed once. Space complexity is O(V) for visited array and queue/stack. Connection to L3: This traversal is adapted to check for a specific target vertex by stopping early when target is found. Common mistakes: not marking visited when enqueuing (causing duplicates), forgetting that graphs can be directed, and stack overflow in DFS recursion for large graphs. Real code pattern: def bfs_traversal(adj, start): visited=[False]*V; q=deque([start]); result=[]; while q: node=q.popleft(); if not visited[node]: visited[node]=True; result.append(node); for neighbor in adj[node]: if not visited[neighbor]: q.append(neighbor); return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: visited=[F,F,F,F], q=[0], result=[]. Step 2: pop 0, **if not visited** mark visited[0]=T (prevents duplicates), result=[0], neighbors [1,2], q=[1,2]. Step 3: pop 1, mark visited[1]=T, result=[0,1], neighbors [3], q=[2,3]. Step 4: pop 2, mark visited[2]=T, result=[0,1,2], q=[3]. Step 5: pop 3, mark visited[3]=T, result=[0,1,2,3].

Step 1: visited=[F,F,F], q=[0], result=[]. Step 2: pop 0, mark visited[0]=T, result=[0], neighbors [1], q=[1]. Step 3: pop 1, mark visited[1]=T, result=[0,1], neighbors [2], q=[2]. Step 4: pop 2, mark visited[2]=T, result=[0,1,2], neighbors [0] but visited[0]=T, skip, q=[]. Return result.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation вЂ” Graph Representation and Basic Terminology
ladders.desc: Understand vertices, edges, adjacency lists, and directed graphs
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding what a graph is and how to represent it computationally. A graph consists of vertices (nodes) and edges (connections between vertices). In a directed graph, edges have a direction: an edge from vertex u to vertex v means you can travel from u to v, but not necessarily vice versa. The adjacency list representation is common: for each vertex, we maintain a list of vertices it connects to via outgoing edges. For example, if vertex 0 has edges to vertices 1 and 2, its adjacency list is [1,2]. This representation is space-efficient for sparse graphs. Key terminology: vertex (node), edge (directed/undirected), adjacency, reachability (ability to travel from one vertex to another via edges). Intuition: Graphs model relationships where entities (vertices) are connected (edges). Connection to L4: This representation is used as input for graph traversal algorithms. Common mistakes: confusing 0-based and 1-based indexing, misunderstanding directed vs undirected edges, and incorrect adjacency list construction. Real code pattern: To build adjacency list from list of edges: def build_adjacency(V, edges): adj=[[] for _ in range(V)]; for u,v in edges: adj[u].append(v); return adj. Example: V=3, edges=[[0,1],[1,2]] gives adj=[[1],[2],[]].

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize adj as list of 3 empty lists: [[],[],[]]. Step 2: Process edge [0,1]: append 1 to adj[0], now adj[0]=[1]. Step 3: Process edge [1,2]: append 2 to adj[1], now adj[1]=[2]. Step 4: adj[2] remains []. Final: [[1],[2],[]].

approaches.name: Tarjan's Algorithm O(V + E)
approaches.summary: Single DFS that maintains discovery times, low-link values, and stack to identify SCCs using back edges. Construct condensation graph by tracking edges between SCCs during the DFS. More efficient in practice than Kosaraju.

ladders.title: Full Problem вЂ” Build Condensation Graph Using Tarjan's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to find SCCs and construct DAG of SCCs from a directed graph.
ladders.explanation: This level solves the complete problem: given a directed graph, find all strongly connected components (SCCs) using Tarjan's algorithm, then construct the condensation graph where each SCC becomes a node and edges connect SCCs that have connections in the original graph. The intuition is that Tarjan's algorithm performs a single DFS while maintaining discovery times, low-link values, and a stack to identify SCCs when a node's low-link equals its discovery time. During this process, we track edges between different SCCs to build the condensation graph efficiently. The algorithm works because low-link values propagate through back edges to identify cycles, and the stack ensures we correctly group nodes belonging to the same SCC. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize arrays for discovery times, low-link values, stack status, and SCC IDs. 2) Perform DFS from each unvisited node. 3) During DFS, update discovery and low-link values, push nodes to stack. 4) When encountering a back edge to a node in the stack, update low-link to min of current low-link and that node's discovery. 5) When a node's low-link equals its discovery time, pop nodes from stack until reaching this nodeвЂ”all popped nodes form one SCC. 6) While processing edges during DFS, if an edge goes to a node in a different SCC (already assigned), record this edge between SCCs. 7) After DFS completes, we have SCC assignments and edges between them, which form the condensation DAG. Time complexity is O(V+E) for the DFS and edge processing. Space complexity is O(V+E) for adjacency lists, arrays, stack, and condensation graph storage. Connection to higher levels: This integrates all sub-routines into a complete solution. Common mistakes include not resetting low-link values correctly for back edges, forgetting to check if a node is in the stack when updating low-link, or incorrectly handling multiple edges between the same SCCs in the condensation graph. Real code pattern: Use recursion for DFS with global time counter, stack list, and arrays for disc, low, on_stack, and scc_id.

examples.trace: Start DFS from node 0: disc[0]=0, low[0]=0, push 0. в†’1 (disc[1]=1, push 1) в†’2 (disc[2]=2, push 2). From 2 в†’0 (back edge, on_stack): low[2]=min(2, disc[0])=0. Backtrack в†’ low[1]=0. From 1 в†’3: disc[3]=3, low[3]=3, push 3. low[3]==disc[3] в†’ pop 3 as SCC1=[[3]]. Backtrack to 0: low[0]==disc[0] в†’ pop 2,1,0 as SCC0=[[0,1,2]]. Edge 1в†’3 creates condensation edge [[0,1]].

DFS from 0: disc[0]=0, low[0]=0, push 0. в†’1 (disc[1]=1, push 1) в†’2 (disc[2]=2, push 2) в†’3 (disc[3]=3, push 3) в†’4 (disc[4]=4, push 4). From 4 в†’2 (back edge, on_stack): low[4]=min(4, disc[2])=2. Backtrack в†’ low[3]=2, low[2]=2. low[2]==disc[2] в†’ pop 4,3,2 as SCC2=[[2,3,4]]. Back to 1: low[1]==disc[1] в†’ pop 1 as SCC1=[[1]]. Back to 0: low[0]==disc[0] в†’ pop 0 as SCC0=[[0]]. Edges 0в†’1 and 1в†’2 create condensation edges [[0,1],[1,2]].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine вЂ” Tarjan's DFS for SCC Identification
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive DFS that finds SCCs using discovery times, low-link values, and stack.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core DFS function in Tarjan's algorithm that identifies SCCs. The intuition is that during a DFS traversal, we assign each node a discovery time (when it's first visited) and a low-link value (the smallest discovery time reachable from that node via its descendants and back edges). When we find a node where low-link equals discovery time, it means no back edge connects this node to an ancestor, so it's the root of an SCC. We use a stack to track nodes in the current DFS path; when we identify an SCC root, we pop nodes from the stack until we reach this rootвЂ”all popped nodes belong to the same SCC. This works because low-link values propagate through back edges: if a node can reach an ancestor via a back edge, its low-link gets updated to that ancestor's discovery time, preventing it from being an SCC root. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize global time counter, stack, and arrays for disc, low, on_stack, scc_id. 2) For each unvisited node, call DFS. 3) In DFS: set disc[node]=low[node]=time++, push node to stack, mark on_stack[node]=true. 4) For each neighbor: if unvisited, recurse, then low[node]=min(low[node], low[neighbor]); if visited and on_stack[neighbor], low[node]=min(low[node], disc[neighbor]). 5) After processing neighbors, if low[node]==disc[node], pop stack until node is popped, assign same scc_id to all popped nodes. Time complexity per node is O(1) for array operations plus O(degree) for neighbors, totaling O(V+E). Space is O(V) for arrays and stack. Connection to L0: This sub-routine finds SCCs; L0 adds edge tracking for condensation graph. Common mistakes: forgetting to update low-link when neighbor is on stack (using disc[neighbor] not low[neighbor]), or not marking on_stack false when popping. Real code pattern: recursive function with parameters node, adj_list, and global arrays.

examples.trace: Start DFS from 0: disc[0]=0, low[0]=0, push 0. Go to 1: disc[1]=1, low[1]=1, push 1. Go to 2: disc[2]=2, low[2]=2, push 2. From 2 в†’ 0 (back edge, on_stack): low[2]=min(2, disc[0])=0. Backtrack в†’ low[1]=0, low[0]=0. At node 0, low[0]==disc[0], pop stack until root 0: pop 2, then 1, then 0, assign same scc_id.

DFS from 0: disc[0]=0, low[0]=0, push 0. Go to 1: disc[1]=1, low[1]=1, push 1. Go to 2: disc[2]=2, low[2]=2, push 2. Go to 3: disc[3]=3, low[3]=3, push 3. From 3, go to 1: 1 is visited and on_stack, low[3]=min(3, disc[1])=1. Backtrack to 2: low[2]=min(2, low[3])=1. Backtrack to 1: low[1]=min(1, low[2])=1. At node 1, low[1]==disc[1]==1, pop until 1: pop 3,2,1, assign scc_id. Back to 0: low[0]=min(0, low[1])=0, at node 0 low[0]==disc[0]==0, pop 0 as separate SCC.

ladders.title: Core Logic вЂ” Low-Link Value Propagation and SCC Root Detection
ladders.desc: Understand how low-link values identify SCC roots via back edges in DFS.
ladders.explanation: This level explains the central mechanism in Tarjan's algorithm: using low-link values to detect SCC roots. The intuition is that in a DFS tree, an SCC forms a cycle where nodes can reach each other. The low-link value of a node is the smallest discovery time reachable from that node, including via back edges to ancestors. When a node's low-link equals its own discovery time, it means no back edge connects it to an ancestor with a smaller discovery time, so it is the 'root' of an SCC (the first node discovered in that SCC). This works because during DFS, low-link values propagate: when we encounter a back edge to an ancestor in the stack, we update the current node's low-link to that ancestor's discovery time. As we backtrack, we propagate low-link values up the DFS tree using min operations. Eventually, at the SCC root, all nodes in the cycle will have the same low-link value (the root's discovery time), and when we return to the root, its low-link remains equal to its discovery time, triggering SCC formation. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize low[node] = disc[node] when first visited. 2) When exploring neighbor: if unvisited, recurse, then low[node] = min(low[node], low[neighbor]). 3) If neighbor is visited and in stack (back edge), low[node] = min(low[node], disc[neighbor]). 4) After processing all neighbors, if low[node] == disc[node], node is SCC root. Time complexity for this logic is O(1) per edge for updates. Space is O(1) per node for low array. Connection to L1: This logic is embedded in the DFS function to identify when to pop SCCs. Common mistakes: using low[neighbor] instead of disc[neighbor] for back edges (which can incorrectly propagate low values across SCCs), or not checking if neighbor is in stack for back edge detection. Real code pattern: in DFS, after recursive call, update low[node] with low[child]; for back edge, update with disc[neighbor] if on_stack.

examples.input: Node 0 with disc=0, neighbors: 1 (disc=1), back edge to ancestor with disc=0
examples.trace: Start at node 0: disc[0]=0, low[0]=0. Go to neighbor 1: disc[1]=1, low[1]=1. From 1, encounter back edge to 0: 0 is visited AND on_stack, so low[1]=min(1, disc[0])=0. Backtrack to 0: low[0]=min(0, low[1])=0. Now low[0]==disc[0]==0, so 0 is SCC root.

examples.input: Node 2 with disc=2, neighbors: 3 (disc=3), and back edge to 1 (disc=1) in stack
examples.trace: At node 2: disc[2]=2, low[2]=2. Go to neighbor 3: disc[3]=3, low[3]=3. From 3, no back edges, so backtrack: low[2]=min(2, low[3])=2. Also, from 2, back edge to 1: 1 is in stack with disc=1, so low[2]=min(2, disc[1])=1. Now low[2]=1, disc[2]=2, so not equalвЂ”2 is not an SCC root.

testCases.input: V=1, edges=[], disc[0]=0, low[0]=0, no back edges
disc[1]=1, low[1]=0 after back edge to disc=0
disc[2]=2, low[2]=2, back edge to disc=1
disc[3]=3, low[3]=3, back edge to disc=3
disc[6]=6, low[6]=4 from back edge to disc=4
disc[7]=7, low[7]=7, back edge to disc=5 not in stack
disc[9]=9, low[9]=6 from child low=6 and back edge to disc=7

ladders.title: Building Block вЂ” DFS Traversal with Discovery Times and Stack Management
ladders.desc: Perform DFS on a directed graph, tracking discovery times and using a stack for path recording.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the foundational DFS traversal needed for Tarjan's algorithm, focusing on assigning discovery times and managing a stack to track the current path. The intuition is that DFS explores nodes recursively, and we assign each node a discovery time (the order in which it's first visited) to understand the exploration sequence. The stack is used to keep nodes that are in the current DFS path (i.e., nodes that have been visited but not yet fully processed and assigned to an SCC). This helps identify back edges: an edge to a node in the stack indicates a cycle. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize a time counter starting at 0. 2) For each unvisited node, call DFS. 3) In DFS: mark node as visited, set disc[node] = time++, push node onto stack, mark on_stack[node] = true. 4) For each neighbor: if unvisited, recurse; if visited and on_stack, it's a back edge. 5) After processing all neighbors, the node will be popped from stack later when SCC is identified. Time complexity is O(V+E) for DFS traversal. Space is O(V) for visited array, disc array, stack, and on_stack array. Connection to L2: This traversal provides the disc values and stack context needed for low-link propagation. Common mistakes: not incrementing time correctly, forgetting to mark on_stack false when popping, or using a visited set that doesn't distinguish between fully processed and in-stack nodes. Real code pattern: iterative or recursive DFS with stack list and boolean array on_stack.

examples.trace: Start DFS from 0: time=0, disc[0]=0, push 0, stack=[0]. Go to neighbor 1: time=1, disc[1]=1, push 1, stack=[0,1]. Go to neighbor 2: time=2, disc[2]=2, push 2, stack=[0,1,2]. Node 2 has no neighbors, so return. Later, when SCCs identified, stack popped. Final disc: [0,1,2].

DFS from 0: time=0, disc[0]=0, push 0, stack=[0]. Go to 1: time=1, disc[1]=1, push 1, stack=[0,1]. From 1, go to 0: 0 is visited and on_stack (since in stack), so back edge detected. Continue, later stack popped when SCC found.

ladders.title: Basic Operation вЂ” Graph Representation and Adjacency List Iteration
ladders.desc: Represent a directed graph using adjacency lists and iterate over neighbors.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental operation of representing a graph and accessing its edges, which is essential for DFS traversal. The intuition is that graphs can be represented in multiple ways; for Tarjan's algorithm, an adjacency list is efficient because it allows O(1) access to neighbors and overall O(V+E) time. An adjacency list is an array or list of lists, where index i contains a list of nodes that i points to. This compact representation saves space for sparse graphs. Step-by-step: 1) Given number of nodes V and list of edges, initialize an empty list of size V. 2) For each edge uв†’v, append v to the list at index u. 3) To iterate over neighbors of node u, loop through the list at adj[u]. Time complexity for building is O(E), and iterating neighbors is O(degree(u)). Space complexity is O(V+E) for storing the lists. Connection to L3: This representation is used in DFS to explore neighbors. Common mistakes: using adjacency matrix (O(VВІ) space), not handling multiple edges correctly, or assuming undirected graph. Real code pattern: in Python, use list of lists: adj = [[] for _ in range(V)]; for u,v in edges: adj[u].append(v).

examples.trace: Initialize adj = [[], [], []]. For edge 0в†’1: append 1 to adj[0], so adj[0]=[1]. For edge 1в†’2: append 2 to adj[1], so adj[1]=[2]. adj[2] remains []. Final adj: [[1], [2], []].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation вЂ” Directed Graphs and Cycles
ladders.desc: Understand directed graphs, edges, and the concept of cycles.
ladders.explanation: This level introduces the basic concepts needed for SCCs: directed graphs and cycles. A directed graph consists of nodes (vertices) and directed edges (arrows from one node to another). An edge uв†’v means you can go from u to v, but not necessarily vice versa. A cycle is a path that starts and ends at the same node, following the direction of edges. For example, if there is a path 0в†’1в†’2в†’0, that's a cycle. Cycles are important because SCCs are maximal sets of nodes where each node can reach every other node, which inherently involves cycles. The intuition is that in a directed graph, nodes in the same SCC are mutually reachable, forming a cycle or a set of interconnected cycles. Step-by-step: 1) Define nodes as integers 0 to V-1. 2) Define edges as pairs (u,v). 3) A cycle exists if starting from a node, you can follow edges and return to it. 4) SCCs group nodes that are in cycles together. Time and space are not analyzed here as it's conceptual. Connection to L4: This foundation is used to build graph representations. Common mistakes: confusing directed with undirected graphs, or not understanding that reachability must be mutual for SCCs. Real code pattern: no code here, just understanding.

examples.trace: Start at node 0: follow edge to 1. From 1, follow edge to 2. From 2, follow edge back to 0. This forms a cycle (0в†’1в†’2в†’0) в†’ all three nodes are mutually reachable в†’ they form one SCC.
Start at 0: go to 1, then 2, then 3. From 3, there are no outgoing edges, so cannot return to 0 or any previous node. Thus, no cycle exists.
